title: Mating rabbits and managing gestation
subtitle: Taking the doe to the buck, confirming mating, and preparing for kindling.
excerpt: Rabbits breed readily, but the process needs managing carefully to avoid injury and ensure a good environment for the litter. Gestation lasts 31 days, and the nest box goes in at day 28.
sourceNotes: British Rabbit Council breed standards and husbandry guidance. RWAF small-scale breeding advice. Merck Veterinary Manual, rabbit reproduction section.

glossaryTerms.definition: An adult female rabbit. Does are territorial and should always be taken to the buck's cage for mating, not the reverse.
An adult male rabbit. In a mating session the buck is left in his own cage because he is more confident on familiar ground, which reduces stress and improves mating success.
The act of a doe giving birth. Kindling usually happens at night and is typically over within 30 minutes. Most does need no assistance.
A enclosed box placed in the doe's hutch from day 28 of gestation. The doe lines it with hay and pulled fur. Kittens are born blind and hairless and depend on the nest box for warmth.

content.text: Rabbits can breed from as young as 4 months. Most keepers wait until the
doe
is at least 6 months old and in good body condition. A thin or overweight doe has a higher risk of complications at
kindling
. Rabbits do not have a heat cycle in the same way as many other mammals. Does are induced ovulators and can mate at almost any time of year.
Taking the doe to the buck
Always take the
doe
to the
buck
's cage. Never put the buck into the doe's space. A doe in her own territory is likely to attack the buck. Bucks on their own ground are calmer and more confident.

Stay nearby and watch the session. Mating is quick. After a successful mount the buck usually falls sideways or backwards. This is normal. Most keepers allow two to three successful matings, then remove the doe.
Remove the doe after 10 minutes even if no mating has occurred. Prolonged contact risks fighting. If the doe is not receptive, try again the following day.
Confirming mating

There is no reliable at-home pregnancy test for rabbits. You can gently palpate the abdomen from day 12 to 14. Small marble-sized swellings in a row indicate developing foetuses. This takes practice and should be done gently. Pressing too hard can cause miscarriage.

A simpler check is to offer the doe to the buck again on day 14. A pregnant doe will usually refuse the buck and may attack him. A doe who mates again was probably not pregnant from the first session.
Gestation length

Gestation in rabbits is 31 days, with a normal range of 28 to 35 days. Mark the date of mating and count forward. Litters born before day 28 are usually not viable. Litters carried past day 35 may indicate a problem.
Nest box provision at day 28
Place a
nest box
in the doe's hutch at day 28. The box should be large enough for the doe to enter and turn around, with sides high enough that kittens cannot fall out. Fill it with plenty of meadow hay.
The doe will begin pulling fur from her chest and flanks to line the nest. This is a strong sign that kindling is close. Do not disturb the nest once she has started lining it.
After kindling

Check the nest box on the first morning. Count the kittens and remove any that are dead or stillborn. Do not handle live kittens longer than needed. The doe will not reject her young because of human scent, but prolonged disturbance can cause her stress.

Does nurse once or twice a day, usually at night. An absence at the nest is normal. Check that the kittens' bellies look round and full rather than pinched. Pinched bellies suggest the kittens are not getting enough milk. If this persists by day 3, seek vet advice.
